findings.title: Brief gives March 14 incident date, but case file gives March 12
findings.description: The location is corroborated, but the brief states the incident occurred on March 14, 2021. The medical records, police report, and witness statement all state the incident occurred on March 12, 2021, which directly conflicts with March 14.

evidence.quote: This action arises from a workplace incident on March 14, 2021, at a commercial construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Location:          2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, CA 90006
the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief states March 14, while three independent source documents identify March 12 for the same location and incident. This is a mechanically checkable date contradiction.

findings.title: Fact 3 gives March 14 incident date, but case file gives March 12
findings.description: The sources support that Rivera was working on scaffolding when a section gave way, but they contradict the date. All three source documents identify March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 3

evidence.quote: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Patient was working on scaffolding at a commercial construction site when the structure gave way.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
A section approximately 12 feet in width had given way.
On the morning of March 12, 2021, our crew arrived at the site at approximately 7:00 AM.
The entire section he was on came down.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief states the scaffolding collapse occurred on or about March 14, while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all date the same collapse to March 12. The matching scaffolding-collapse facts make the date conflict unambiguous.

findings.title: Limitations section gives March 14 incident date, but case file gives March 12
findings.description: Every source document that dates the accident states March 12, 2021. That directly contradicts the brief's March 14, 2021 date.

evidence.quote: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
DATE OF ADMISSION:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021

I, Diane Tran, provide the following statement of my own free will regarding the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief's limitations argument states March 14, while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all identify March 12. The conflict is direct and mechanically checkable.

findings.title: Brief says Rivera lacked required PPE, but case file says he wore PPE and a harness
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment. The police report and witness statement directly state he was wearing a hard hat and harness, and Tran adds a high-visibility vest, all consistent with site PPE requirements.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 4

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief directly denies required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, while both the police report and witness statement say Rivera wore a hard hat and harness. The harness-lanyard details further corroborate the contradiction.

findings.title: Brief says there is no rebuttal evidence, but file contains safety-defect evidence
findings.description: The brief asserts an absence of evidence, but the supplied file contains police observations and Tran's statement identifying rust, worn components, unstable footing, and pre-incident safety concerns. These are evidence of potential safety failures and therefore contradict the blanket assertion that Rivera presented no rebuttal evidence.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishes a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
The base plates on the failed section appeared to be resting on uneven ground, with one plate sitting on a loose plywood sheet rather than a stable footing.
Officers observed that portions of the scaffolding in the affected section showed visible rust and wear.

Tran further stated that she and other crew members had raised concerns about the condition of the east-side scaffolding earlier in the week, noting that some of the cross-braces "didn't look right" and that the base on the far end "was sitting on a piece of plywood that kept shifting."
I noticed several things that concerned me:
Some of the cross-braces on the lower tier appeared to have significant surface rust and looked like they had been reused from a previous job.
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing.
Two of the coupling pins on the second tier looked bent or worn.
The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief's categorical 'no evidence' assertion is contradicted by multiple source quotes describing unstable footing, rust, worn pins, and prior safety complaints. The only uncertainty is legal—whether that evidence would be sufficient to rebut the asserted presumption—but it is plainly some evidence.

findings.title: Brief's no-Harmon-responsibility framing conflicts with evidence of Harmon direction and safety involvement
findings.description: Apex is corroborated as Rivera's employer, but the claim that Apex—not Harmon—was responsible for scaffolding operations omits and conflicts with evidence that Harmon foreman Ray Donner directed the crew to work on the east-side section and dismissed a safety concern about the base plate. Those facts undercut the brief's framing that Harmon had no operational role.
findings.brief_location: III.A. Harmon Is Not Liable Under the Privette Doctrine

evidence.quote: Here, the undisputed facts establish that Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Employer:      Apex Staffing Solutions
Ray Donner, the Harmon Construction Group project foreman, was present on site at the time of the incident.
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.
Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
During our morning coordination meeting, Ray Donner â€” the project foreman for Harmon Construction â€” told us that the east-side scaffolding section needed to be fully operational by end of day.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner. Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
I want to note that Donner had personally directed us to work on that section that morning and dismissed my concern about the base plate.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The file quotes show Harmon foreman Donner directing the crew to work on the east-side section and allegedly dismissing a base-plate safety concern, which materially conflicts with the brief's 'Apex—not Harmon' operational-responsibility framing. Some uncertainty remains because Apex's employer status is corroborated and responsibility could be legally divided.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette appears doctored
findings.description: Privette v. Superior Court is a real and well-known California Supreme Court case, but the quoted absolute language is not faithful to its doctrine. The suspect word is “never”: Privette addressed peculiar-risk/vicarious liability for contractor-employee injuries, not all possible hirer liability for injuries arising from contracted work. Known actual language/rule: Privette held that a hirer is generally not vicariously liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to an independent contractor's employees, who are covered by workers' compensation. It did not announce an absolute “never liable” rule; later Privette-line cases recognize liability where the hirer’s own conduct, such as retained control that affirmatively contributes to the injury, is involved.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted absolute 'never liable' formulation conflicts with the well-known Privette rule and its recognized retained-control and related exceptions. Confidence is high but not absolute because the evidence does not include the actual Privette page text for verbatim comparison.

findings.title: SeaBright does not establish an OSHA-compliance presumption
findings.description: SeaBright supports the Privette/delegation principle, not the proposition that a hirer's compliance with statutory safety requirements is 'highly probative' of due care or that an OSHA inspection record insulates the hirer from tort liability. The brief converts SeaBright's delegation holding into an OSHA-compliance evidentiary presumption that the case does not establish.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

findings.confidence_reasoning: SeaBright is a canonical Privette-line delegation case, and the described OSHA-compliance presumption is not its holding. The confidence is capped because this rests on legal knowledge of the case rather than a supplied full-text excerpt.

findings.title: Authority does not support the stated Privette proposition
findings.description: The quoted statement is overbroad and is not an accurate statement of the Privette doctrine. California law recognizes exceptions to the Privette presumption, including retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazards, and related exceptions developed in cases such as Hooker, Kinsman, McKown, SeaBright, and later cases. Thus the authority supports only a narrower presumption of nonliability, not a categorical 'never liable' rule.

evidence.quote: Id. at 702.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The Privette doctrine is well known and does not state a categorical 'never liable' rule, but this finding is duplicative of the more specific doctored-quote finding and its own evidence excerpt only shows the pinpoint citation, not the challenged quoted language.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: I do not recognize this case, and there are concrete fabrication signals: 887 F.2d is not consistent with a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation as I understand the Federal Reporter chronology, the quoted OSHA-compliance 'rebuttable presumption' sounds like a conveniently tailored rule, and I am not aware of such a Ninth Circuit/California negligence rule. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would be at most persuasive, not binding, on a California Superior Court's application of California negligence law.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991) ("Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.").

findings.confidence_reasoning: The reporter-year mismatch and highly tailored OSHA presumption provide concrete fabrication signals, but the record does not include an external citation search or reporter table confirming nonexistence. Confidence is therefore moderate rather than mechanical.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co.
findings.description: I do not recognize this federal district case, and there are concrete fabrication signals: the case name and parenthetical fit the brief's scaffolding/Privette facts unusually closely, and it is an obscure district-court citation used to supply a fact-specific summary-judgment holding. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a C.D. Cal. district-court decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California state tort law.

evidence.quote: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004) (granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade).

findings.confidence_reasoning: The citation has suspiciously tailored facts and no located authority is supplied, but the finding rests largely on non-recognition rather than a documented database search or contradictory reporter evidence. It is kept because a fabricated case citation, if confirmed, would be a serious misrepresentation.

findings.title: Elapsed-time calculation conflicts with the sourced incident date
findings.description: No source verifies the complaint filing date, but the elapsed-time calculation is contradicted by the incident date in the sources. From March 12, 2021 to March 12, 2022 is 365 days, and from March 12, 2022 to March 10, 2023 is 363 days, for 728 days total—one year and 363 days, not one year and 362 days.

evidence.quote: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023 â€” one year and 362 days after the incident.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: Using the sourced March 12, 2021 incident date and the brief's stated March 10, 2023 filing date, the elapsed time is 728 days, not the brief's stated one year and 362 days. Severity is reduced because the arithmetic discrepancy itself does not appear to alter the two-year limitations outcome.

findings.title: Time-barred heading is undermined by the section's concession that filing is nominally within the limitations window
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations (CCP 335.1 / two years = 730 days). Filing on 2023-03-10 is 726 days after the incident on 2021-03-14, which is inside the 730-day window. The time-barred framing is therefore self-defeating.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The heading says the claims are time-barred, while the body expressly concedes the filing is nominally within the limitations window, creating a clear misleading-framing issue. Confidence is not higher because the same sentence tries to preserve an accrual-date challenge, so the contradiction is partly argumentative rather than purely factual.

Because the specific experience duration is a record-checkable qualification not found in the file, the full claim is unsupported.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted assertion includes a specific eight-year experience duration that the checker reports is absent from the source documents. Confidence is limited because the evidence excerpt shows the assertion but not all searched sources establishing the absence.

Because those qualifications are material parts of the asserted fact and are not documented in the file, the full claim is unsupported.

Confidence is limited because the evidence list shows only the brief quote and not the full negative search across the source documents.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The filing date is a concrete litigation fact, and the checker reports no complaint or docket document among the supplied sources. Confidence is moderate because the adjudication evidence contains only the brief assertion, not the full document inventory proving absence.

findings.title: Legal arguments advanced without supporting authority
findings.description: The brief states and applies rules of law without citing any case, statute, or regulation in support. Uncited assertions: (1) at III.C (second paragraph): Under the doctrine of assumption of risk, a plaintiff who voluntarily encounters a known danger cannot recover for injuries arising from that danger. (2) at III.C (third paragraph): As a trained professional, Rivera understood the hazards of scaffolding work and accepted them as part of his employment. (3) at III.D (first paragraph, last sentence): Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

The defect is plausible and material, but not as mechanically verifiable from the provided quote alone.

judicial_memo: Overall, the brief contains several material factual conflicts with the supplied record and significant problems in its use of authority. Most prominently, the brief repeatedly states that the incident occurred on March 14, 2021—in the Introduction, Fact 3, and the limitations section—while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all identify March 12, 2021. The brief also asserts that Rivera lacked required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, but the police report and witness statement state he wore a hard hat and harness, with Tran also noting a high-visibility vest. Its assertion that Rivera presented no rebuttal evidence conflicts with file materials describing rust, worn components, unstable footing, and pre-incident safety concerns. The Privette quotation appears overbroad, particularly the “never liable” formulation, and SeaBright is characterized as creating an OSHA-compliance presumption it does not establish. Two cited authorities could not be located in available materials, and several factual assertions lack record support.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that under California law, a hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.quoted_text: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that the California Supreme Court held that a hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.full_citation: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004) (granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade).
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that summary judgment should be granted to a hirer where the subcontractor controlled the scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.case_name: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
citations.full_citation: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991) ("Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.").
citations.quoted_text: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that compliance with applicable OSHA standards entitles an employer to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.case_name: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
citations.full_citation: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that the California Supreme Court recognized that compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of the exercise of due care.

citations.full_citation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 335.1
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that under California Code of Civil Procedure Section 335.1, the statute of limitations for personal injury claims is two years.

citations.full_citation: Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp., 198 Cal.App.4th 223 (2011);
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief cites Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp. as additional authority supporting the proposition stated in footnote 1 (compliance with statutory safety requirements/related negligence due care point).

citations.full_citation: Blackwell v. Sunrise Contractors, Inc., 45 Cal.App.4th 1012 (1996);
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief cites Blackwell v. Sunrise Contractors, Inc. as additional authority supporting the proposition stated in footnote 1 (compliance with statutory safety requirements/related negligence due care point).

citations.case_name: Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC
citations.full_citation: Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC, 387 S.W.3d 154 (Tex. App. 2012);
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief cites Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC as additional authority supporting the proposition stated in footnote 1 (compliance with statutory safety requirements/related negligence due care point).

citations.full_citation: Okafor v. Brightline Builders, Inc., 291 So.3d 614 (Fla. Dist. Ct. App. 2019);
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief cites Okafor v. Brightline Builders, Inc. as additional authority supporting the proposition stated in footnote 1 (compliance with statutory safety requirements/related negligence due care point).

citations.full_citation: Nguyen v. Allied Pacific Construction Co., 112 Cal.App.4th 845 (2003);

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), is a well-known California Supreme Court decision and the reporter/year are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that a person who hires an independent contractor generally is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to the contractor's employees, in light of workers' compensation and delegation principles. Later California cases developed this into the modern Privette presumption, subject to exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition is broadly consistent with the Privette doctrine as later described: a hirer is presumptively not liable for injuries to an independent contractor's employees arising from the contracted work. The brief should not omit that the presumption is rebuttable and subject to recognized exceptions, including retained control that affirmatively contributes to injury, concealed hazards, and some situations involving defective equipment or other hirer conduct.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: This is an id. citation to the real Privette decision; page 702 is within the reported decision.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette rejected imposing peculiar-risk liability on a hirer for injuries to an independent contractor's employee. It did not announce an absolute rule that a hirer is never liable in all circumstances.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The quoted statement is overbroad and is not an accurate statement of the Privette doctrine. California law recognizes exceptions to the Privette presumption, including retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazards, and related exceptions developed in cases such as Hooker, Kinsman, McKown, SeaBright, and later cases. Thus the authority supports only a narrower presumption of nonliability, not a categorical 'never liable' rule.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this federal district case, and there are concrete fabrication signals: the case name and parenthetical fit the brief's scaffolding/Privette facts unusually closely, and it is an obscure district-court citation used to supply a fact-specific summary-judgment holding.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify that the case exists and there are fabrication signals, I cannot assess whether it supports the asserted proposition.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a C.D. Cal. district-court decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California state tort law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this case, and there are concrete fabrication signals: 887 F.2d is not consistent with a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation as I understand the Federal Reporter chronology, the quoted OSHA-compliance 'rebuttable presumption' sounds like a conveniently tailored rule, and I am not aware of such a Ninth Circuit/California negligence rule.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because the case appears likely fabricated and I cannot verify it, I cannot assess support. Separately, the asserted rule is suspect as a matter of California law: regulatory compliance may be relevant evidence, but it generally does not automatically create tort immunity or a broad presumption of due care.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would be at most persuasive, not binding, on a California Superior Court's application of California negligence law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: SeaBright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), is a well-known California Supreme Court Privette/Cal-OSHA decision and the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: SeaBright held that a hirer presumptively delegates to an independent contractor any tort-law duty to provide a safe workplace, including duties imposed by Cal-OSHA regulations, and therefore the hirer was not liable to the contractor's employee for the contractor's failure to comply with those safety duties.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: SeaBright supports the Privette/delegation principle, not the proposition that a hirer's compliance with statutory safety requirements is 'highly probative' of due care or that an OSHA inspection record insulates the hirer from tort liability. The brief converts SeaBright's delegation holding into an OSHA-compliance evidentiary presumption that the case does not establish.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a real California statute governing personal-injury limitations periods.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for actions for assault, battery, injury to, or death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition that California personal-injury claims generally have a two-year statute of limitations is supported. However, the brief's broader time-bar argument appears internally weak because a March 10, 2023 filing for a March 14, 2021 injury is within two years on the facts stated.
